text: Ah, another week, another episode of The
Last of Us to get through. Oh my
god. And I say it that way because
that's exactly what this show is
starting to feel like for me. Homework.
Some mildly unpleasant tasks to be
completed as quickly as possible instead
of a relic and good piece of
entertainment that I can't wait to
experience week after week. And
apparently I'm not the only one to feel
this way as the show's lost almost 20%
of its audience by episode 3 and its
Rotten Tomato score has plummeted to
just 49% officially marking it as rotten
for the first time. Golly G whiz. I
wonder what could have possibly caused
this. I mean that really got out of hand
fast. It jumped up a notch. It did,
didn't it? The thing is almost everyone
knew this was coming. Everyone who
played the games knew that killing off
Joel was going to put the show into a
death spiral that it would struggle to
pull out of as the narrative devolved
into a grimly sadistic misery simulator
where every major character dies or ends
up broken and alone. Everyone who
watched season 1 could have told you
that Bella Ramsay's Ellie wasn't going
to be able to carry a show like this
without Pedro Pascal there to support
her. Everyone knew this was coming
except for HBO apparently.
You know, they say the definition of
insanity is doing the same thing again
and again and expecting different
results. And if that's the case, then I
think Neil Duckman needs an intervention
pronto because in technical
terminology, he's alone. But from a
writing point of view, I think it's
actually worth explaining why killing
off Joel was such a massive mistake both
in the games and in the show. Because
this ties into a much bigger issue about
character relationships and the
importance of having balance and
contrast in your stories. And the
reality here is that without Joel, The
Last of Us has no emotional heart
anymore. See, the first game and to some
extent the first season of The Last of
Us both successfully tapped into one of
the most compelling and universal
character dynamics in all of fiction.
The bond between a father and his
daughter. Yeah, it was definitely an
unconventional version of that
relationship. Ellie wasn't really Joel's
daughter, and they didn't even meet each
other until she was an adolescent, but
the allimportant dynamic was still the
same. Joel was the experienced protector
and father figure, and Ellie was the
young and vulnerable daughter that he
had to guide, protect, and watch over.
He was jaded, cynical, and pragmatic,
hardened by a lifetime of tough choices
and brutal survival, while she was
idealistic and maybe a touch naive,
eager to see the world without knowing
much about how dangerous it could really
be out there. Obviously, she was no
match for him physically because he was
a grown man and she was a little girl.
And if anything, she's actually a burden
on him at first. She doesn't know much
about hunting or survival. She makes a
lot of mistakes that almost get them
both killed. And despite all her
[ __ ] and bravado, she's actually
pretty scared and intimidated by the
world around her. It made for a great
contrast, allowing them to spark off
each other, provoke and challenge each
other, and grow and develop in
interesting ways. Because of Joel, Ellie
gradually learns how to look after
herself, how to survive in a harsh and
unforgiving world to the point where she
even has to step up and become a
protector for him when he's badly
wounded. And because of Ellie, Joel's
demeanor slowly softens as he warms to
her and starts to see her as more than
just a package that has to be delivered
to a destination. He begins to see her
as someone he genuinely cares about, a
surrogate for the daughter that he lost
20 years earlier. Joel brings out
Ellie's inner strength while Ellie
brings out Joel's humanity. In a neat
piece of narrative give and take, they
each fill gaps in the other's life. The
show mostly managed to replicate this
dynamic in season 1 despite his best
efforts. And the reason I say that is
that you can tell they did everything in
their power to toughen Ellie up and
break Joel down, bending the dynamic as
far as they could possibly go without
breaking it. The Ellie in the show was
much more confident, assertive, and
capable right from the start. Easily
taking control of conversations and
disagreements. Rarely showing any fear
of Joel or the world around them.
Frequently disobeying his instructions
and mouthing off at him as if a
50-year-old man with a gun that she
barely knows is the kind of guy you'd
want to risk pissing off. TV Joel, on
the other hand, is much less decisive,
capable, and stoic than his game
counterpart. Even at the beginning of
their journey together, he suffers from
panic attacks. He's starting to struggle
physically and often seems to be at a
loss for what to do next. He frequently
gets flustered and outmaneuvered in
conversations with Ellie, and he even
defers to her when it comes to making
decisions, as if a 12-year-old girl
who's never been outside the walls of
her hometown is the kind of person you
should listen to in a life ordeath
situation.
I
hate so much about the things that you
choose to be. The subtext here is that
TV show Ellie has a lot less to learn
from Joel than her game counterpart that
she needs him less. There's less of a
contrast between them. So, the
fatherdaughter dynamic is weakened
considerably. It's not destroyed
completely, but it's far inferior to the
game. And that's not even taking into
account all the shameless political
pandering the show indulges in.
Communism. Nah. Nah. It ain't like that.
It is that literally. This is the
commune. We're communists.
This place actually [ __ ] works. You
see kids, if everyone just pulls
together and does their part, communism
really can work for all of us. To find
out more about how you can get involved,
contact your local political commissar.
Now, still, it was enough to keep
audiences happy with season 1 of the
show. That, however, is gone now. Joel's
dead, and instead Ellie is left with
Dena to play off of. And well, that's a
whole different dynamic that's
considerably less interesting. Why?
because there's no contrast between
these two characters. They're about the
same age, the same gender, and roughly
the same level of experience and
competence. Even their personalities are
broadly similar. Yeah, Ellie's a bit
more serious and focused, and Dena's a
bit more outgoing and playful, but
there's really not that much of a
difference between them. Joel and
Ellie's relationship wasn't just a
broken man and a frightened girl trying
to find a way to connect with each
other. It was a clash of different
generations and worldviews. Joel, who
still remembered the old world before
the collapse, and Ellie, who was born
into the new world, but still has to
experience it. Joel's maturity and
experience, versus Ellie's youthful
passion and curiosity. Ellie and Dena
don't have any of that because they're
so similar. It's more like watching a
person talking to themselves. Their
scenes come across as flat, dull, and
tedious, and not just because the actors
don't have much chemistry together.
Neither of them has anything
particularly interesting or insightful
to say. They don't offer contrasting
opinions drawn from different life
experiences. They don't even have
different physical capabilities that
might offer some kind of contrast in how
they approach situations. They're just
two [ __ ] teenagers meandering through
the world swapping shitty jokes and
speculating about stuff that they don't
really understand. And how can I put
this? Teenagers aren't exactly the most
interesting people you'll ever meet. And
for obvious reasons, they haven't seen
and done enough to mold them into
interesting people yet. They're more
like houses that have just been moved
into. Yeah, the potential is there, but
there's none of the interesting
decoration or personality that builds up
over time. In fact, when you think about
it, the three main characters in this
show now are all pretty similar. They're
the same gender, roughly the same age
range, the same size and build, and the
same level of experience. Again, there's
no real contrast between them, and so
there's less to get interested and
invested in. It also means that this
show is badly missing gravitas. Like it
or not, there's a certain kind of
authority and charisma and command that
only older men
possess. Young guys don't have it and
women don't have it. And when you erase
it from a narrative, you lose that sense
of believability and gravity that a show
like The Last of Us is desperately
striving for. It doesn't have it now.
And you won't find it with characters
like Ellie, Abby, and Dena. All of this
is my long-winded way of saying that
killing off Joel was a huge mistake in
the games, and it's likely to be just as
damaging to the TV show. Not only was it
a shitty way to kill off such a popular
character, but his death leaves behind a
narrative hole that nobody else is
capable of filling. They really did try
the same thing as before, and surprise,
surprise, they got the same [ __ ]
result. And they really have no one to
blame but
themselves. Anyway, that's all I've got
for today. Go away now.
